content:
# Leviticus Chapter 20 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)

**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 70r–70v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_020_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_020_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** The LORD spoke to Moses: **2** "Tell the Israelites: Any person — whether Israelite or foreigner living in Israel — who gives his children to Molech must be put to death. The people of the land shall stone him. **3** I myself will set my face against that man and cut him off from his people, because he gave his children to Molech — defiling my sanctuary and profaning my holy name. **4** If the people of the land close their eyes to what that man has done — if they look the other way when he gives his children to Molech and they fail to put him to death — **5** then I will set my face against that man and against his family. I will cut him off, and all who prostitute themselves after him by chasing after Molech, I will cut them off from their people.

**6** "If anyone turns to mediums or spiritists and prostitutes himself after them, I will set my face against that person and cut him off from his people.

**7** "Consecrate yourselves and be holy, because I am the LORD your God. **8** Keep my statutes and carry them out. I am the LORD who makes you holy.

**9** "Anyone who curses his father or his mother must be put to death. He has cursed his own father and mother — his blood is on his own head.

**10** "If a man commits adultery with another man's wife — with the wife of his neighbor — both the adulterer and the adulteress must be put to death.

**11** "If a man sleeps with his father's wife, he has violated his own father's honor. Both of them must be put to death — their blood is on their own heads.

**12** "If a man sleeps with his daughter-in-law, both of them must be put to death. What they have done is a perversion — their blood is on their own heads.

**13** "If a man lies with a man as with a woman, both of them have done something abominable. They must be put to death — their blood is on their own heads.

**14** "If a man takes both a woman and her mother, that is depravity. All three must be burned with fire so that there is no depravity in your midst.

**15** "If a man has sexual relations with an animal, he must be put to death, and you must kill the animal.

**16** "If a woman approaches any animal to mate with it, both the woman and the animal must be put to death. Their blood is on their own heads.

**17** "If a man takes his sister — whether his father's daughter or his mother's daughter — and they have sexual relations, that is a shameful thing. They must be publicly cut off from their people. He has violated his own sister — he will bear the guilt of it.

**18** "If a man sleeps with a woman during her monthly period and has sexual relations with her, he has exposed the source of her flow and she has uncovered it. Both of them must be cut off from their people.

**19** "Do not have sexual relations with your mother's sister or your father's sister, for that is to violate a close blood relative — both will bear the guilt.

**20** "If a man sleeps with his uncle's wife, he has dishonored his uncle. They will bear the guilt — they will die childless.

**21** "If a man marries his brother's wife, it is an act of impurity. He has violated his brother — they will be childless.

**22** "Keep all my statutes and all my ordinances and carry them out, so that the land where I am bringing you to live will not vomit you out. **23** Do not walk in the statutes of the nations I am driving out before you. They did all these things, and that is why I despised them. **24** But I said to you: you will possess their land — I will give it to you, a land flowing with milk and honey. I am the LORD your God, who has set you apart from all the peoples.

**25** "You must therefore distinguish between clean and unclean animals, and between clean and unclean birds. Do not defile yourselves through animals or birds or anything that moves along the ground — creatures I have set apart for you as unclean. **26** Be holy to me, because I, the LORD, am holy. I have set you apart from the peoples so that you would be mine.

**27** "Any man or woman who is a medium or a spiritist must be put to death. They are to be stoned — their blood is on their own heads."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### The Mirror of Prohibition: Chapter 20 and Chapter 18

Leviticus 18 and Leviticus 20 form a diptych. Chapter 18 lists what is forbidden; chapter 20 states what happens when the forbidden is done. The architecture is deliberate. To read them together is to understand that Leviticus is not presenting arbitrary rules — it is describing a moral order in which acts have consequences, not merely because humans enforce penalties, but because the fabric of community life is either built or destroyed by what its members do in secret.

The penalties in chapter 20 are not graduated on a scale of personal harm alone. They are calibrated to the degree to which an act assaults the structures that make human community possible: family, covenant, the integrity of the sanctuary, and the holiness of the divine name. The death penalty does not appear for every offense — some carry the penalty of *karet* (being cut off), others die childless — but where death is prescribed, it is for acts that most directly strike at the foundations.

### Verses 1–5: Molech and the Invisible Conspiracy

The Molech laws that open chapter 20 are the most severe in the entire chapter, and their severity is instructive. The passage describes not only the perpetrator but those who **look the other way** — who "close their eyes" (*ya'alimu ha-am ha-arets et-'eineihem*). This is remarkable: passive bystanding in the face of child sacrifice is treated as its own category of offense that draws the direct divine response *ani ethen et-panai* — "I will set my face against."

The phrase *ani ethen et-panai* — "I will set my face against" — is one of the most severe formulas in the entire Hebrew Bible. The face of God turned toward someone is a source of blessing (the Aaronic blessing of Numbers 6:24–26 prays for God's face to *shine upon* the recipient); the face of God turned *against* someone is annihilation. This is divine hostility concentrated into a gaze.

Why this severity for Molech? Because child sacrifice strikes at the most fundamental covenant category: the next generation. If the children are burned, the covenant has no future. The line between the Abrahamic promise and the next generation is the body of every child. To offer that child to Molech is not merely a religious transgression — it is the literal destruction of the covenant's future in the present moment.

The inclusion of the foreigner (*ger*) under the Molech prohibition in verse 2 is also significant. The resident foreigner does not escape the standards of holiness because he is not a native Israelite. The land belongs to the LORD, and what is done on the land matters regardless of who does it. The theological principle here runs all the way through to Leviticus 18:25: the land itself becomes defiled by what happens on it, and the land will vomit out its inhabitants — citizen or foreigner — when the defiling acts continue unchecked.

### Verse 6 and Verse 27: The Bookend on Necromancy

The chapter opens by dealing with Molech (vv.1–5) and closes by dealing with mediums and spiritists (v.27). This is not accidental arrangement. Both practices represent the same fundamental transgression: seeking life and power from a source other than the living God. Molech worship offers children in exchange for supposed fertility and prosperity; necromancy consults the dead for guidance and knowledge. Both are acts of spiritual adultery — turning away from YHWH to find what only YHWH can give.

The severity of the final verse (v.27) — stoning for mediums and spiritists — is the same penalty prescribed for Molech worship at the chapter's opening. The structural symmetry makes the point: this is not a minor superstition; it is an alternative covenant, a rival loyalty, a worship of a different kind of power. The community's holiness cannot coexist with it.

### Verses 7–8: The Hinge That Changes Everything

In the middle of a catalog of capital offenses, verses 7–8 interrupt the pattern:

*"Consecrate yourselves and be holy, because I am the LORD your God. Keep my statutes and carry them out. I am the LORD who makes you holy."*

This is the theological heart of the chapter, and it completely reframes what surrounds it. The penalties are not presented as punishments handed down by a vindictive law code. They are presented as the consequences of the contrast between a holy God and an unholy act. The God who speaks these words is the One who *makes* Israel holy — *mekadishkhem*, the sanctifier, the one who actively works holiness into the people. The penalties that surround this hinge exist not to satisfy judicial retribution but to protect what God is actively creating: a holy people.

This is the paradox at the center of Leviticus: the same God who commands severe penalties also says "I am the LORD who makes you holy." Holiness is not primarily Israel's achievement; it is God's work in Israel. The laws exist to protect the space in which that work can occur.

### The Death Formula: *Mot Yumat*

Leviticus 20 uses the emphatic death formula *mot yumat* — "he shall surely be put to death" — repeatedly throughout the chapter (vv.9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 15, 16, 27). This is an infinitive absolute construction in Hebrew: the infinitive of the verb *muth* (to die) followed by the finite form of the same verb. The effect in English would be something like "dying he shall die" — a grammatical doubling that communicates absolute certainty and full legal force.

The formula is found throughout the Pentateuch wherever the most serious violations are described. Its function is both legal (unambiguous prescription) and rhetorical (you cannot read past it without feeling its weight). Dynamic equivalence translation must decide how much of that rhetorical weight to preserve. "Must be put to death" captures the legal force; the repetition within the chapter accumulates into a drumbeat that the reader feels without being able to point to any single word.

### Verse 9: Cursing Parents

"Anyone who curses his father or his mother must be put to death. He has cursed his own father and mother — his blood is on his own head."

The prohibition on cursing parents immediately follows the holiness hinge (vv.7–8). The sequencing is significant: honoring parents is the first specific application of holiness in the social order. This parallels the Decalogue, where "honor your father and mother" is the fifth commandment — the bridge between the duties toward God (commandments 1–4) and the duties toward fellow humans (commandments 6–10). Parents are the conduit through which life and covenant identity are transmitted. To curse them is to curse the transmission of the very life that makes one's own existence possible.

The phrase *damo bo* — "his blood is on him" — appears throughout chapter 20 (vv.9, 11, 12, 13, 16, 27). It is a judicial formula that attributes moral responsibility to the offender: the community that executes the penalty is not guilty of bloodshed; the guilt attaches to the one who committed the offense. The formula exists to relieve the executioners of blood guilt — the killing of a person is ordinarily the most serious offense, but in these cases, the offender's own actions have transferred that guilt to themselves.

### The Sexual Offense Laws (Vv.10–21): Structure and Gradation

The penalties for sexual offenses in verses 10–21 mirror the prohibitions of chapter 18, but they are not mechanically identical in structure. A few features stand out:

**Death for both parties.** In most sexual offenses, the chapter specifies death for both parties. Verse 10 names both adulterer and adulteress; verse 11 prescribes death for both the man and his father's wife; verse 12 for both the man and his daughter-in-law. This is in marked contrast to ancient Near Eastern law codes (including Hammurabi) which often treated the woman as the primary offender and the man as merely complicit. Leviticus 20 is remarkably even-handed: when both parties are willing, both bear equal responsibility.

**Karet — being cut off.** Not all offenses receive death. Verses 17–21 prescribe a different penalty: *karet* (being cut off from the people) or *avam yishan* (dying childless, bearing guilt). *Karet* is one of the most debated terms in biblical law. It clearly involves exclusion from the covenant community, but whether it refers to execution, divine punishment, excommunication, or some combination has been debated by rabbinic interpreters for centuries. Its function in the text is to communicate serious consequence short of immediate physical death — a fate perhaps worse, because the person lives on without covenant belonging.

**Verse 17: A "shameful thing."** The offense of intercourse between a man and his sister is described not as an abomination (*toevah*) but as a *hesed* — a word that normally means "covenant-faithfulness" or "loving-kindness." In this context it appears to be used ironically or in the sense of "disgrace" — a usage attested elsewhere in older Hebrew. The LXX reads it as *asebeia* (godlessness/impiety). This may reflect a scribal tradition that found the normal word *hesed* so dissonant in this context that it required interpretive softening. PNEUMA renders it "a shameful thing" to capture the opprobrium without importing the theological resonances of the more common meaning.

### Verse 14: The Fire Penalty

Verse 14 — the case of a man who takes both a woman and her mother — prescribes death by burning rather than stoning: *ba-esh yisrefu oto ve-ethen*. Fire is used elsewhere in the Pentateuch for violations involving the priesthood (Lev 10:1–2; 21:9) but is rare as a general judicial penalty. Its use here likely signals a particularly acute defilement — the collapse of generational boundaries in a way that is qualitatively different from other sexual offenses. The rabbinic tradition debated whether this referred to burning while alive (extreme) or burning of the corpse after execution (less extreme); the latter became the normative reading.

### Verses 22–26: The Theology of Separation

The final exhortation in verses 22–26 is the theological crown of chapters 18–20. It brings together the key terms and themes that have structured the entire Holiness Code to this point:

**The land vomiting (v.22).** The threat that the land will "vomit out" (*taqih ha-arets*) its inhabitants returns from chapter 18 (vv.25, 28). The image is visceral and cosmological: the land is not a passive territory; it has a moral character, and it responds to what is done on it. To be vomited out is to be expelled by the very ground beneath your feet — to have the very earth reject you.

**The gift of separation (v.24).** The passage reaches its peak at verse 24: *ani YHWH Eloheikhem asher-hivdalti etkhem min-ha-ammim* — "I am the LORD your God, who has set you apart from all the peoples." The verb *hivdil* is the root of *havdalah* — separation. This is the same root used in Genesis 1 for God's creative act of separating light from darkness, waters above from waters below. The separation of Israel from the nations is presented as an act of the same creative, ordering power that structured the cosmos at the beginning. Israel's distinctiveness is not ethnic supremacy; it is a divine act of world-formation, as foundational as the separation of sea from dry land.

**"Be holy, for I am holy" (v.26).** Verse 26 restates the thesis of the Holiness Code (*qedoshim tihyu ki qadosh ani*, from Lev 11:44–45 and 19:2) with one crucial addition: *va-avdil etkhem min-ha-ammim lihyot li* — "I have separated you from the peoples to be mine." The purpose of Israel's holiness is covenantal relationship. Israel is not holy for its own glory or for the purpose of ethnic superiority. Israel is holy *lihyot li* — "to be mine." The holy people belongs to the holy God.

The LXX renders this *kai aphoriaka hymas apo panton ton ethnon einai emoi* — "and I have separated you from all the nations to be mine." The verb *aphorizo* (to separate, set apart, define a boundary) is the same verb Paul uses in Romans 1:1 to describe his own calling: *aphorismenos eis euaggelion theou* — "set apart for the gospel of God." The apostle who will declare that in Christ there is neither Jew nor Gentile (Gal 3:28) describes his own vocation with the word the LXX used for Israel's separation from the nations. The New Testament writers are steeped in this language.

### The Clean and Unclean Animals (Verse 25): Embodied Holiness

The instruction to distinguish between clean and unclean animals in verse 25 — sandwiched between the sexual offense laws and the final holiness statement — may seem like an intrusion. But it is deliberately placed. The same act of *havdalah* (separation/distinction) that describes Israel's separation from the nations also describes the Israelite's relationship to the animal world. To eat discriminately — not because some animals taste worse, but because the God who called you to holiness has called you to practice acts of distinction in every domain of life — is itself a formative practice.

The Jewish philosopher Emmanuel Levinas observed that the dietary laws train a form of attention: you cannot eat thoughtlessly. Every meal becomes an act of consciousness, a moment in which the fundamental categories of holy and common, clean and unclean, are enacted in the body. The laws of chapter 20, for all their severity, are doing the same thing at a larger scale: they are mapping the categories of distinction onto the entire social body of Israel, so that the community itself becomes — meal by meal, boundary by boundary — a living argument for the existence of a God who makes distinctions.

### The LXX in Vaticanus (Pages 106–107)

Codex Vaticanus preserves the Greek of Leviticus 20 with characteristic faithfulness. Several translation choices deserve attention:

The Molech prohibition in verse 2 is rendered with the LXX's standard transliteration of the name: *Molokh* — a form that appears throughout the LXX and enters the New Testament in Acts 7:43, where Stephen's speech quotes the prophet Amos on Israel's Molech worship in the wilderness. The chain from Leviticus to Amos to Acts runs directly through the LXX.

The phrase *ani ethen et-panai* — "I will set my face against" — becomes in the LXX *ego episto to prosopon mou* — "I will set/fix my face." The Greek *prosopon* (face, presence, person) carries the same weight as the Hebrew *panim*: the face of God is the concentrated presence of God, directed as either blessing or judgment.

The *karet* penalty in several verses is rendered *exolothreuthesontai* — "they will be utterly destroyed" — a compound verb with the prefix *ek-* (out of, completely) intensifying *olothreuo* (to destroy). The LXX translators were clearly treating *karet* as something close to divine destruction rather than merely communal excommunication. This is the "cutting off" that comes from above, not merely from human judgment.

Verse 26's *lihyot li* — "to be mine" — becomes in Greek *einai emoi* — "to be mine." The possessive is direct, intimate, unmediated. The God who separates does not separate for abstract theological purposes; he separates for relationship. The final word of the separation theology is not *distinction* but *belonging*.

---
*PNEUMA — translated from Leningrad Codex (B19A) folios 70r–70v, with reference to Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) pages 106–107 (LXX).*
